content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

March 22, 2017

Michael Torres
Assistant City Attorney
City of Newport Beach
100 Civic Center Drive
Newport Beach, CA 92660

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-17-061

Dear Mr. Torres:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Newport Beach City
Councilmember Jeff Herdman, regarding his duties under the conflict of interest provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of
interest or Section 1090.

QUESTION

May Councilmember Herdman participate in the following governmental decisions:

(1) The repair or replacement of the Balboa Island sea walls?

(2) The dredging of the Grand Canal?

(3) Sand replenishment on Balboa Island's beaches?

CONCLUSION

Based on your facts, the exception to the materiality rules for a decision that "solely
concerns repairs, replacement or maintenance of existing streets, water, sewer, storm drainage or
similar facilities" applies and Councilmember Herdman does not have a conflict of interest.

_____________________________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-17-061
Page No. 2

FACTS

Balboa Island consists of three picturesque islands: Balboa Island, Little Balboa Island, and
Collins Isle (collectively, "Balboa Island"). According to the 2010 census, Balboa Island has
approximately 2,111 homes and 2,756 residents, but Balboa Island is used and enjoyed by all City
residents, and visitors from throughout the state, nation, and world. The islands are linked to the
mainland and to each other by a combination of bridges and ferry service. The City will be
considering the following matters regarding Balboa Island.

Repair or Replacement of the Balboa Island Sea Walls: Balboa Island is protected from the
waters of Newport Harbor by approximately two linear miles of City-owned sea walls. The sea
walls were first built in the 1920's and 1930's and are now approaching or exceeding their useful
life expectancy. The City is at the beginning stages of engaging the community in developing
options to reconstruct or replace the aging sea walls. Since his election to the City Council, out of
an abundance of caution, Councilmember Herdman has recused himself from participating on any
items related to the Balboa Island sea walls, because they are located within 500' from at least one
of his homes.

Grand Canal Dredging. Balboa Island and Little Balboa Island are separated by a waterway,
commonly referred to as the Grand Canal. The Grand Canal is navigated by small boats (kayaks,
duffy boats, etc.) traversing the waters of Newport Harbor. From time-to-time, there is sediment
build-up within the Grand Canal, which obstructs vessel passage. As a result of this sediment build-
up, the City engages in regular dredging of the Grand Canal. Since his election to the City Council,
out of an abundance of caution, Councilmember Herdman has recused himself from participating
on any items related to the dredging of the Grand Canal, because it is located within 500' from at
least one of his homes.

Beach Sand Replenishment. The perimeter of Balboa Island is comprised of public sandy
beaches that are owned by the City and used by the public. Occasionally, natural tidal action washes
away the sand from these beaches, which requires the City to place additional sand on the beaches
to prevent them from disappearing. Since his election to the City Council, out of an abundance of
caution, Councilmember Herdman has recused himself from participating on any items related to
sand replenishment on Balboa Island's beaches, because they are located within 500' from at least
one of his homes.

The Councilmember's Interests: Councilmember Herdman was elected to the Newport
Beach City Council at the November 8, 2016 General Election. For the past 62 years,
Councilmember Herdman has been a resident of the City living on Balboa Island. Currently,
Councilmember Herdman has an ownership interest in two homes on Balboa Island; one home is
located on Balboa Island and the other home is located on Little Balboa Island.

Prior to his service on the City Council, Councilmember Herdman had been an active
community participant in the Balboa Island sea wall discussion, serving as President of both the
Balboa Island Improvement Association ("BIIA") and the Little Balboa Island Property Owners'
Association ("LBIOA"), and serving as a volunteer citizen advisory member of the City's former
Tidelands Management Committee ("TMC").

File No. A-17-061
Page No. 3

ANALYSIS

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials "perform their duties in
an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the financial interests
of persons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).) Specifically, Section 87100 prohibits
any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her official
position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. The only
interest implicated by your account of the facts is an interest in real property in which an official
has a direct or indirect interest worth $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b).)²

Foreseeability and Materiality

Generally, a financial effect is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the interest is
"explicitly involved" in a decision. An interest is "explicitly involved" in a decision if the interest is
a named party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision before the official or the official's
agency. (Regulation 18701(a).) Real property is the subject of a decision when "the decision
involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other
entitlement to, or contract with, the financial interest, and includes any governmental decision
affecting a real property financial interest as described in Regulation 18702.2(a)(1)-(6).

If an interest is not explicitly involved in the decision, a financial effect is reasonably
foreseeable if the effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or
theoretical. If the financial result cannot be expected absent extraordinary circumstances not subject
to the official's control, it is not reasonably foreseeable. (Regulation 18701(b).)

Different standards apply to determine whether a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on
an interest will be material depending on the nature of the interest. Regulation 18702.2 defines
when a financial effect of a government decision on real property is material.

However, Regulation 18702.2(c) provides an exception for a decision that "solely concerns
repairs, replacement or maintenance of existing streets, water, sewer, storm drainage or similar
facilities." In prior letters, we have applied the exception (in a prior iteration) in the following
cases:

• Stepanicich Advice Letter, No. A-14-053: We found the exception to apply to a flood
control project that was intended to achieve a nationally accepted standard of flood
protection formulated to protect life and property. The various flood control measures that
were considered for the project included stabilizing levees, clearing channels, widening
channels, modifying bridges, and constructing floodwalls, detention basins and an
underground bypass culvert.

² To the extent that Councilmember Herdman has other interest implicated in the decision including but not
limited to any business interest operating from either of the properties, you should seek further advice identifying the
interest.

File No. A-17-061
Page No. 4

• Stovall Advice Letter, No. A-08-112: We found that a project to dredge a 14-mile slough was a "storm drainage or similar facility" and that the dredging of the silted-up slough was a repair or maintenance activity.

• Cauble Advice Letter, No. I-06-179: We found the exception to apply where a flood control project to remove roots and vegetation from a slough would increase its flood control capacity. Because the slough's clogging was caused by flood control measures taken twenty years earlier, the project was meant to repair, replace, or maintain the slough's storm drainage capacity as it once existed.

Under the facts you have provided, none of the decisions described appear to grant the councilmember "new and improved services" but are rather repair, replace or maintain existing facilities. Accordingly, the decisions you have described meet the terms of the exception and therefore the decisions would not have a material financial effect on the councilmember.

Please be aware, however, that as the process proceeds if the decisions should change to ones that involve "new and improved services" rather than the repair, replacement and maintenance of the existing facilities, you should contact us for further advice.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

By: John W. Wallace
Assistant General Counsel
Legal Division
